104
    See generally Roger Bellet, Presse et Journalisme sous le Second Empire (Paris: Armand Colin, 1967),
18-24 (law of 1868) and passim.
105
    Discussion in Contamine-Raynaud, Le secret de la vie privée, in L’Information en droit privé (Paris:
LGDH, 1978), 406. Contemporary commentary did worry over protecting the private life of the President
of the Republic, though. See J. Gahier, La Diffamation et la Loi du 29 Juillet 1881 (Paris : Librairie
Générale de Jurisprudence, 1893), 80.

36
 “la vie privée”—for example, in forbidding a writer to base a novel on events revealed in

the course of a criminal trial. 106 All told, France was, by the 1870s and 1880s, the home

of a very visible law of the protection of privacy. 107 The reigning French view was

captured by a characteristic account that appeared in 1888, two years before the

publication of Warren and Brandeis’ “Right to Privacy.” In that year, Émile Beaussire,

an eminent legal philosopher of the day, summarized several decades of French in the

following way. The right to privacy was to be ranked among the rights to honor more

broadly. This of course raised doubts about its place in the law. For understandably,

Beaussire wrote, people viewed their “honor” as something that should be preserved

through dueling, not through law: “When my honor is attacked, I gain nothing by filing

suit against my calumniators.”108 Nevertheless, in the modern world, even persons of

honor had to have recourse to the law, and the law had to occupy itself with persons of

honor. This was the right context for understanding the right to privacy: For violations

of “privacy” involved nothing other than the revelation of “infamous secrets” which

could destroy the honor of such persons as a respected “père de famille.”109 And outside

the most exceptional circumstances, the law could not allow such violations:

Private honor, whatever its value per se and whatever its source, must be

protected from all offenses. People have certainly railed against the excesses of

the maxim “private life must be walled off” . . . [Nevertheless], the prying and

insults of the world may be . . . more or less innocent or more culpable : no matter

106
    Le Figaro c. Chaperon, Annales de la Propriété Industrielle, Artistique et Littéraire 45 (1899), 61-65.
107
    Not all French commentators approved of this. As one leading scholar argued, for example, French law
was beginning to trench too drastically on the work of artists. The “right to one’s image,” he thought,
should not extend beyond the traditional protection against “insult and defamation.” Vaunois, Liberté du
portrait, 6.
108
    Émile Beaussire, Les Principes du Droit (Paris: Alcan, 1888), 369: “Quand mon honneur est attaqué, je
ne gagne rien à poursuivre en justice mes calomniateurs.”
109
    Beaussire, Principes du Droit, 372.
